content.content:
1	You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
2	checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
3	and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.
4	
5	## Your job
6	
7	Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
8	Nothing else.
9	
10	## What to look for
11	
12	Focus on these four buckets, in this order:
13	
14	1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
15	   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
16	2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
17	   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
18	   boundaries.
19	3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
20	   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
21	4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
22	   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.
23	
24	Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
25	musings, hypothetical future requirements.
26	
27	## How to gather context
28	
29	- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
30	- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input. Don't use
31	  `git diff` — the checkout is shallow (`fetch-depth: 1`) so comparisons
32	  against main have no merge base and will fail.
33	- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
34	  changed lines.
35	
36	### Verifying external facts
37	
38	Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
39	library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
40	Look it up before flagging it.
41	
42	- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
43	  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
44	  Prefer this over web search for docs.
45	- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
46	  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
47	  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
48	  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.
49	
50	If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
51	question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.
52	
53	## How to post feedback
54	
55	### Inline comments (primary)
56	
57	Specific issues go in inline comments via
58	`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
59	One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
60	comment must include:
61	
62	- A one-line description of the issue.
63	- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
64	- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
65	  change. Be concrete; reference the code.
66	
67	Severity rubric:
68	- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
69	- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
70	- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
71	- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.
72	
73	### High-level overview (optional)
74	
75	If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
76	else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
77	via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
78	sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
79	have nothing to add at that level. Escape any backticks in the `--body` string as `\`` to avoid shell interpretation.
80	
81	If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
82	"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.
83	
84	## When you're done
85	
86	Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner will exit.
87

file.content:
You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.

## Your job

Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
Nothing else.

## What to look for

Focus on these four buckets, in this order:

1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
   boundaries.
3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.

Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
musings, hypothetical future requirements.

## How to gather context

- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input. Don't use
  `git diff` — the checkout is shallow (`fetch-depth: 1`) so comparisons
  against main have no merge base and will fail.
- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
  changed lines.

### Verifying external facts

Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
Look it up before flagging it.

- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
  Prefer this over web search for docs.
- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.

If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.

## How to post feedback

### Inline comments (primary)

Specific issues go in inline comments via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
comment must include:

- A one-line description of the issue.
- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
  change. Be concrete; reference the code.

Severity rubric:
- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.

### High-level overview (optional)

If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
have nothing to add at that level. Escape any backticks in the `--body` string as `\`` to avoid shell interpretation.

If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.

## When you're done

Stop immediately after your last tool call. Do not list your findings, do not recap which comments you posted, do not confirm the comment count. The runner will exit.

content.content:
1	name: PR Review agent
2	
3	on:
4	  pull_request:
5	    types: [opened, ready_for_review, reopened]
6	    paths-ignore:
7	      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
8	      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
9	  workflow_dispatch:
10	
11	jobs:
12	  review:
13	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
14	    permissions:
15	      contents: write
16	      pull-requests: write
17	      id-token: write
18	      actions: write
19	      checks: read
20	      statuses: read
21	    steps:
22	      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
23	        with:
24	          fetch-depth: 1
25	
26	      - id: prompt
27	        run: |
28	          {
29	            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
30	            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
31	            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
32	            echo
33	            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
34	            echo PROMPT_EOF
35	          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
36	
37	      - id: claude
38	        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
39	        with:
40	          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
41	          track_progress: false
42	          # senior/junior PRs are now opened by the App bot, which the action
43	          # would otherwise reject as a bot-initiated event. Set
44	          # AGENT_APP_BOT_LOGIN to the App's bot login (e.g. "narcie-dev[bot]").
45	          allowed_bots: ${{ vars.AGENT_APP_BOT_LOGIN }}
46	          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
47	          settings: |
48	            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
49	          claude_args: |
50	            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"
51	
52	      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
53	        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
54	        env:
55	          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
56	          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
57	          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
58	          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
59	        run: |
60	          set -e
61	          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
62	          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
63	          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
64	          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
65	          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
66	          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
67	          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
68	          git add "$DEST"
69	          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
70	          git push origin "$BRANCH"
71	
72	      - name: Trigger agent improver
73	        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
74	        env:
75	          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
76	          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
77	          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
78	          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
79	        run: |
80	          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
81	            -f agent_name=pr-review \
82	            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
83	            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"
84

file.content:
name: PR Review agent

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, ready_for_review, reopened]
    paths-ignore:
      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
      checks: read
      statuses: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - id: prompt
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
            echo
            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          track_progress: false
          # senior/junior PRs are now opened by the App bot, which the action
          # would otherwise reject as a bot-initiated event. Set
          # AGENT_APP_BOT_LOGIN to the App's bot login (e.g. "narcie-dev[bot]").
          allowed_bots: ${{ vars.AGENT_APP_BOT_LOGIN }}
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"

      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin "$BRANCH"

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
            -f agent_name=pr-review \
            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this run carefully:

The run summary shows:
1. Agent started with `gh pr view 65 --json ...` - successful
2. Agent ran `gh pr diff 65` - successful
3. Agent ran `ls -la /home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/` - to check if `bin/` exists
4. Agent ran `ls /home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/.github/ && ls /home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/bin 2>/dev/null || echo "bin/ does not exist"` - to verify the bin/ directory
5. Agent searched for the inline comment tool and posted the comment

The workflow was efficient—the agent gathered the PR details and diff, identified that the spec references a `bin/` directory that doesn't actually exist, verified this with filesystem checks, and then flagged it with an inline comment. Good practice to confirm the issue before reporting it.

I'm noticing something odd though: the allowed tools list specifies `Bash(gh pr comment:*)`, `Bash(gh pr diff:*)`, and `Bash(gh pr view:*)`, but the agent also ran generic `ls` commands to check the directory structure. Those `ls` calls shouldn't have been allowed based on the tool restrictions, yet they executed successfully. The `track_progress: false` setting likely auto-approves permissions, which would explain why the `ls` commands ran successfully despite not matching the `Bash(gh pr comment:*)` allowlist pattern.

Looking at what actually happened in the run, the agent made some redundant calls—it ran `ls` twice to verify the directory structure, first checking the top-level directories and then drilling down into the `bin/` folder specifically. This is a bit wasteful since both calls were essentially confirming the same thing, though the first gave a broader view and the second was more targeted.

The ToolSearch call for the inline comment tool is also worth noting. The prompt already specified the exact tool name to use, so searching for it seems unnecessary—but it turns out this was likely needed because the tool's schema wasn't loaded yet. The ToolSearch was actually loading the deferred tool's schema, which is the expected pattern in this environment.

Looking at the `ls` calls, they're a bit redundant but the agent's approach was sound: it saw `bin/` mentioned in the spec, wanted to verify it existed, ran a broad directory listing, then did a more targeted check. This could've been condensed into a single command with error handling, but it's not worth flagging in the prompt.

Overall the run was clean—the agent used the right tools, verified facts before flagging issues, posted one well-formed inline comment, and stopped immediately after as instructed. It didn't post a generic approval comment or try to do anything else.

I'm noticing the `allowedTools` list shows `Bash(ls:*)` isn't explicitly included, yet the `ls` commands ran successfully with actual output shown. This suggests either the `Bash` permission is more permissive than the allowlist indicates, or the harness auto-approves these commands in this mode.

Since `Read`, `Grep`, and `Glob` are already available and could've accomplished the same verification more efficiently, the agent's choice to use `ls` is slightly suboptimal. Overall though, this was a clean run—the agent gathered PR metadata, did minimal verification, posted a well-formed finding, and stopped. The only minor inefficiencies are the two `ls` calls when one targeted call would suffice and the `ToolSearch` for the inline comment tool, but neither warrants a workflow change.

Wait, I'm realizing I need to reconsider something about the `ls` commands. Looking at the allowedTools list, `ls` isn't explicitly included—only specific Bash commands like `gh pr comment`, `gh pr diff`, and `gh pr view` are whitelisted. If `ls` isn't in the allowlist, it shouldn't have executed. This is puzzling. Maybe in this GitHub Actions context, unlisted Bash commands either get blocked or require approval, but since there's no interactive user in Actions, something else might be happening.

Actually, I'm probably overthinking this. The run clearly produced results, so the commands executed. The real question is whether this reveals any concrete weakness that would make the next run more efficient. Looking at the overall performance—the agent found a real issue, used appropriate verification, and stopped at the right point—I don't see a significant flaw. The two `ls` calls are slightly redundant, but that's minor. This run is actually a good example of the agent working correctly, so no prompt changes seem necessary. I don't see any concrete weakness that would justify changing the approach.

content.text:
The run was clean and efficient. The agent:
1. Got PR metadata and diff in 2 calls using the correct `--json` flags
2. Spotted that `bin/` was referenced in the spec but might not exist
3. Did a quick filesystem check (2 `ls` calls — slightly redundant but both returned useful info)
4. Posted one well-formed HIGH-severity inline comment
5. Stopped immediately after the tool call, exactly as instructed

The minor redundancy (two `ls` calls where one targeted check would do) doesn't rise to the level of warranting a prompt change — a single `ls bin/ 2>/dev/null || echo "..."` would have saved one call, but this pattern is hard to prescribe in a prompt without becoming overly specific. The ToolSearch for the inline comment MCP tool is also expected for deferred tools and not worth annotating.

Nothing concrete to suggest from this run.
